title: Kyle Javier -Hiring Manger Interview - Operations | Account Strategist
meeting_title: Kyle Javier -Hiring Manger Interview - Operations | Account Strategist

transcript.text: Oh, no worries.
I was two minutes late, so we're good.
How is your Monday going?
It's good.
This meeting is being recorded.
It just started.
I'm on the West Coast.
So, yeah.
Nothing so far.
How about yourself?
Pretty good.
So we are your first thing on a Monday.
That is one way to start a week.
It is.
Yeah.
Looking forward to it, especially after my conversation with Tucker.
Awesome.
Yeah.
So I can give you a little bit.

Actually, maybe it would be helpful to understand kind of like your process with Tucker so far, what he's mentioned to you about the role that I can tell you a little bit about myself and how I see the role.
We can jump into your background and then I'll leave time at end for questions.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's a great starting point.
So I had an opportunity to engage with Tucker, Tucker, reached out to me on LinkedIn about this.
Yeah.
Account strategist role.
I'm currently in the market right now as I was unfortunately laid off a couple of weeks ago.
So I thought the timing was right.
So yeah, we had a pretty frank conversation about the technology, the company itself.
I think what really excited me was just the growth of the organization, especially within the last year itself.
And yeah, so that's kind of where we are today.
You know, I dived a little bit deeper, you had some pretty amazing logos on the website, was able to watch a couple of videos and understand a little bit about the technology.
But from my understanding, the account strategist role is somebody that companies lean on, will work together closely and implementing the solution.
They don't necessarily have access to it.
It's not necessarily, it's not a SaaS platform just yet.
But I'll be that middle person, kind of like from a service standpoint, helping them and optimizing their investments.
And...
And...
And...
And, in the operations.
Perfect.
Yeah.
That's, that's basically correct.

So I, just to give you a bit of background on me and the role that I'll play in this cycle is I lead customer operations, which means that I manage kind of like the process and all of the pieces that go into the client deliverables, personnel, and also like, helping to build out the feedback loop with our, engineering team.
Because, as Tucker may have also mentioned, the platform, it's not client facing it, and it's really brand new.
So it's been around for about five weeks, six weeks, live at this point.
And before that, we were building workflows and air ops, leveraging different AI platforms.
Our platform, our internal platform today does still leverage like multiple AI solutions and pulls them in at different points to kind of So, yeah.
So,
Get the optimal, like, use the model that's best at each stage to generate, like, really quality content at scale for our customers.
This role is, I mean, I think we don't want to call it a CSM because we don't have the motion nailed down of, like, of kind of, like, what a CS, what it would look like.
And there's still a lot of technical feedback and alignment that we need to do on what a repeatable client motion looks like from on a day-to-day basis.
So we're, I think we've had one person that started as product ops and kind of moved into this role in his first six months and was super successful.
So we're looking to bring another person like that on.
We're looking for someone who's, like, super gritty, scrappy, hungry.
And willing to kind of build something from the ground up and dive in on the technical pieces of what makes good content through AI prompting and leveraging different tools.

Also, somebody who has some experience on the analytic side, just in terms of things like SEMrush, SEO testing, and you don't necessarily have to have had like a marketing background from that perspective, but just someone who's used to like looking at data to tell a story and run experiments.
So lots of different pieces to that, but, you know, it's at its heart, I think we'll grow into what the CSM role at GrowthX looks like.
But for now, our clients are still sort of special flowers.
And so we need somebody who's going to be willing to work with.
What they need, adapt and experiment, both on the platform and in terms of deliverables for with our clients.
Does that make sense?
Yeah, no, totally does.
Perfect.
So why don't we jump into your background, give me your like elevator pitch, maybe three or five minute overview, and we'll go from there.
Yeah, for sure.
So I mentioned Tucker, I'm a seasoned customer success account management sales professional.
I've been in the technology space.
Specifically SaaS within the past 10 years.
I've had an opportunity to work with a variety of sizes, small to medium businesses, mid market, large enterprise, all the way to large enterprise.
And in all aspects of the customer facing role, I think that's what makes me unique is that I'm more of a jack of all trades.
I had an opportunity to be like a solutions consultant, to be in sales, business development, as well as customer success.
My last organization I worked with, also.
Focused on AI, but more so on enterprise search, where I actually had to build trusted relationships, lead cross-functional discussions, as well as tailored solutions to help with business outcomes.

I think my philosophy, like, I don't think, but my philosophy when it comes to customer success, while it's always to retain customers as well as grow customers, it's to turn these organizations into advocates, whether that's through stories, references, or product co-creation.

I know you mentioned that, like, it's not your typical CSM role, or it's not labeled as a CSM, but I feel quite confident in being able to be that middle person, especially as of right now, between an organization and the technology that we're touching.
Perfect.
Well, let's dive into that part first, in terms of, like, bridging that gap.
Can you tell me about a time that you led a cross-functional project, working with different teams, to...
Get a client what they needed.
Yeah, yeah.
So I work with quite strategic accounts.
I think it might vary specifically for growthx.
But Bass Pro Shops was a massive account.
It's a major Coveo customer, most mature but challenging customer.
They really pushed the boundaries within Coveo and asked a lot of our organization.
So they had access to the customer success manager, who I was, and several product managers.
For machine learning, for merchandising, two members of the Sushan Architect team, our technical account manager, and account manager, and access to our CEO.
And so I had to work very closely with each of them, especially during the account planning session.
There was a new CIO in place specifically for Bass Pro.
And so I instantly knew that we had to win this individual over because I was told by my champions that he has been thinking about building search in-house.
And something that he's done in his past role as well at Lowe's.
So, you know, knowing this, I...
Escalated this internally, shared my concerns, organized in business review.
I actually went on site to Springfield, Missouri to help present this.
But in terms of engaging with different stakeholders, I organized the deck from scratch.
collaborated with everyone that was going on site, the product manager, the account manager, as well as the CEO.
Collaborated with my day-to-days.
Based off everyone's feedback, I fine-tuned the deck itself and engaged on that on site.
You know, it was a very positive on-site.
It was well worth it.
turned the account from red to green.
And we ended up A-B testing three more machine learning models, which ended up succeeding.
The variant was much better than the control.
So large language models, natural language processing, as well as listing page optimizer, which re-ranks the category pages.
And then on top of that, we got a little stickier with new rollouts with the Bass Pro Canada, as well as Max Perry Wings, which is another website that they just recently acquired.
What was your role specifically like at each stage within that?
Yeah, so I was the middle linchpin within this organization.
So I had to provide constant updates.
We ended up putting this organization into hypercare.
So I met with the customer on a daily basis, received their feedback, had to share it internally into our Slack channel.
The Slack channel had a lot of visibility into it.
Roughly 15% of employees were in the Slack channel, including my CEO.
And then in addition to that, creating that deck.
So understanding the value and capturing that value, as well as the product roadmap and the product vision and where we're going.
Those were, I end up having to absorb everything from everybody and create that deck.
And...
In addition to that, I had to do a lot of triage.
I had to organize the onsite as well.
But yeah, like overall, I was the main individual from the Coveo standpoint, but as well as Bass Pro side of things.
So who was the decision maker for the different like deliverables that you ended up getting to Bass?
When you were saying like the internal decision maker or yeah.
Yeah.
So it was, I was working closely with my director and product manager, but the final say was my CEO.
So he wanted to ensure that everything that we shared was going to land.
He is quite specific when it comes to even the transitions, the content, how it looks, et cetera, especially because we're meeting with the CIO and we want to make a great impression.
This is a large strategic customer, especially from an adoption standpoint.
So yeah.
I had to work very closely with the CEO in this case in 700 people.
So at Coveo, did you mostly manage enterprise level clients?
And what was like the makeup of an account team for that?
Yeah, it varied depending on how strategic this accounts were.
But yeah, it was mainly enterprise clients.

On the biggest side of things, it was like there was a technical account manager, dedicated solution architects, there were CEOs, account managers, CSM, so myself, as well as several product managers.
On the lower end, it was just like a CSM.
So I had to wear multiple different hats.
I was a solution architect and answered multiple different questions.
I had to escalate issues from a support perspective, and as well as give root cause analysis in terms of issues as well.
So yeah, I was, depending on the account itself, I was wearing multiple different hats.
Yeah, can you tell me about a time when you owned an account end-to-end as the DRI for client satisfaction?
Yeah, so end-to-end, I worked with Agilent Technologies, which is a, they work on instruments in the healthcare space.
And then they're like a, they're an expansion of Hewitt-Packard.
And so I worked very closely with them once they signed.

And from an onboarding perspective, I created a curated training plan, onboarding session, worked with them end-to-end and just really understanding what their challenges and needs are, leveraged that as a baseline in terms of value realization.
From there, they ended up adopting Coveo, quite a complex implementation, just because of different technologies that had to be put in place.
But I worked with them very closely.
with them closely.

On a bi-weekly basis, so typically I end up meeting bi-weekly or monthly, depending on the cadence, and having business reviews and business reviews, QBRs and executive business reviews with them, and going from adoption all the way to maturity as well.
So thankfully with Agilent, they thought quite highly within Coveo and then actually had a press release about the partnership as well.
Wow, that's great.
And can you tell me about a time that a client-deliverable was going off track and you had to rescue it?
Yeah, Herbalife in particular was very complex.
It was like a three-phased approach across like 94 countries.
They wanted to launch three different websites.
They used a strategic partner for implementation, Deloitte.
They ended up firing them, and so I had to like come in into the middle.
This is before, so let
Throughout Coveo in 2024, we went from having an onboarding team to no onboarding team.
And so they've actually wanted CSMs to lead onboarding.
And so in this case, I jumped right in in the middle of onboarding.
And so I've had to create a little bit more structure when there was none, introduce a success plan and account plan as to where we could go.
They trusted me to the point that they actually gave me a Herbalift.com email address.
So I was able to map out exactly what that org chart looked like.
ended up engaging with like 35 individuals across different business units.
And so because of that, I was able to really showcase the value and understand what the goals were.
But yeah, I had to, there was a lot to it.
Very complex organization, slow moving pieces.
If you're not in the current PI session, you're going to have to move towards the next one, which could be like three to four lines.
So I was always trying to get ahead of the game.
Yeah.
In terms of adoption.
Okay.
Yeah, that sounds tricky.
So when you're working with a client, how do you know when your deliverable is done?
And how do you measure success with the client?

In our case, it's an ongoing situation, but I'll always try to A-B test and tinker with every organization, because there's a lot of different configurations you can have with machine learning models, right?
There's not one size fits all.
In this case, there's machine learning models for popularity.
So clicks, click ranks, things like that.
There's personalization, machine learning models, natural language processing, large language models.
So overall, in the grand scheme of things, I'm always trying to showcase value during business reviews or value realization sessions.
It's always great because now that I'm involved in onboarding, there's baseline information.
So I can see the natural growth.
They did trust me with Google Analytics.
So I was able to show like quarter over quarter and year over year growth within their own platform.

But within that, as I mentioned, I'm always going to try to A-B test the configuration, whether that's increasing the max amount of products that can have been influenced with machine learning models or the score itself.
So it's a constant tinker.
And I've seen with Deckers in particular, so I worked with Aghoka and Teva brands.
And so if you were to go on their site and purchase any of those shoes, it was powered by Coveo.
And so I'd always tinker with the strength of the machine learning model to the point that they saw 15% year over year growth for average order value specifically.
Yeah.
Wow.
Okay.
Just taking notes.
notes.
Okay, and then when you're like juggling a lot of deadlines or managing multiple client accounts, like how do you prioritize your day or your week?
What tools do you use?
Yeah, I'm always about efficiency, right?
Like I can imagine, especially like in like an early age startup, there's probably so many moving pieces that are going on, I need to ensure that my time is being maximized.
So whatever tools are available, in my case, there was, you know, I leveraged actually our LLMs quite often, we had ChatGPT Pro to help out with that.
So just if I'm trying to be a thought leader, especially being a little bit more proactive within accounts.
knows.
They just
It's been a godsend when it comes to capturing that type of information and sharing that type of info within customers.
Can you give me an example of that?
Yeah.

So within Liquor Control Board of Ontario, which is the largest purchaser of liquors globally, just because it's owned by a government, I would highlight specifically what the industry was looking like, what UX experiences also look like for what are trending UX experiences, just so that we can highlight those type of, or we could get ahead of that.
And so they could potentially get that on the roadmap from adoption.
And yeah, so it was really showcasing what the other, what other people within their industry are thinking about at that time, specifically for customer experience.
Okay.
But yeah, to answer your question, you know, I've always been a power user.
You know, like I've been in operations at some point, like I was wearing like a sales ops hat.
So like as a business development manager, I've always had to streamline and automate processes.
So like, I like to consider myself like a power user of Slack and like other different technologies that I've used.
So it's really just fine tuning everything to, to my need and just knowing where things are so that I can save time, 5%, 10% time, which compounds day over day to, to, to maximize.
My days.
And so I can focus on the customers themselves.
Okay.
Um, got it.
Um, I think I would love to like take a step back.
I don't, I'm not sure.
I totally understand what Coveo does.
I was just trying to look on their LinkedIn, but like, talk me through like the solution that you were delivering, um, and how you would describe your role there.
Yeah.
So Coveo specializes in enterprise search.
And so, uh, it,
The way that they view themselves is the relevance platform.
And so like on any given website, they power the search box, the result page, and the recommendations that are on, say, like a product description page.

So if you were to type something out, say like shoes or like a type of shoes, I guess in Ugg's case would be like Bondi's or hiking shoes, it would suggest products as well as suggested terms for you.

And then in addition to that, within the result set page, say there's like 30 or 40 products that show up for hiking shoes, it re-ranks them based off how many clicks in a specific algorithm within machine learning to know that like, hey, like these are the most relevant shoes for you.
And so I worked very closely with a lot of brands in tinkering with that algorithm or that configuration to ensure that it's maximized in the best way it can.
In addition to that, I'm sure you've been on like a retail website, there's always like product recommendation.
Items that you may like, items within the category, things that you may consider as you're planning to check out.
Those are all powered by machine learning as well.
Talk to me about how you see that working with what you understand from GrowthX.
My main takeaway from there would be the constant tinkering and A-B testing of whatever we're trying to accomplish, the strategies themselves from the customers.
And so I think there's constant iteration specifically within that and leveraging that kind of that constant configuration, but also in collaboration with the customers and understanding their goals.
And what they're hoping to achieve to get the most ideal, optimal workflow.
The biggest thing for me is to always refine, test, and optimize the best way I can.
Okay.
Cool.
And then talk to me about a time when you maybe didn't have a lot of clarity on what needed to happen next or how to move an account forward and what you did to kind of take action.
Yeah, there's a lot of ambiguity when it comes to accounts that I've worked on.
Sometimes there's, like, at one point there was no account manager, so I had to plan, I had to plan accounts on my own.
In terms of how I like to tier my accounts, you know, there's strategic accounts that have larger ARR, of course, growth potentials, stable accounts.
So,
Map out my own book of business, but then in addition to that, white space where we could potentially grow within the organization.
And what has a large potential, whom I'm necessarily engaging with, what kind of complexity there is within the platform.
So, yeah, I'll do my own success planning within my own book of business.
But then in addition to that, I'll work very closely with my own day-to-days to then communicate to them, like, hey, this is where I think we should potentially go.
This is where I see what fits within your own implementation and get feedback from them.
And I like to actually do that with all my customers right off the bat as an opportunity to, one, introduce myself, but then let them know this is kind of how my mind works.
This is my approach to this relationship.
And then, and then pretty much go from there.
But most importantly, I think value is number one, right?
Like organizations stay with customer, or organization stays with any type of vendor, platform, partner, because of the value that they bring.
And so level setting and understanding what their true business goals are, what business outcomes that they're hoping to achieve, and then mapping that out and going from there.

And hopefully during my continuing conversations with them further down the line, level setting with them, or not level setting, but touching base on these value points that we're hoping to achieve and see if there's any kind of progress, whether that's through a value realization or PBR or executive business review, but see how we've grown from that initial conversation.
Yeah.
Okay.
Well, those are all the questions.
We really rolled through those.
Um, what questions do you have for me?
Yeah.
So.

So knowing that this is like a newer organization, I want to hear your perspective on where you think the company is going, and what that vision looks like, at least from like an account strategist or operations standpoint.

Yeah, I think the most interesting thing about growthx is our service first approach, like that we started as a services company, and then have our sort of like thinking about transitioning into the SaaS space.
I think that was really smart, in terms of like, looking at TAM, because marketing agencies and services, the services industry is like, there's just so much more spend there on a monthly basis.
And so I think, like, it will, it remains to be seen, and this role will probably have a really big role, like play a really big part in shaping.
What that transition looks like from an account management perspective, because right now we are approaching our clients as like as a service, rather than a platform solution.

So, you know, it's weekly meetings, it's driving strategy and thinking about our deliverables as output on behalf of the client, rather than giving the client a tool that they can generate deliverables on.
And so, like, I haven't really done a lot of work in this industry.

I think some of the principles still ring true, but what that transition looks like will really be up to this person to help drive, like, if you're successful in this role, you will be helping me set the strategy for, like, a SaaS launch and revealing our platform to customers and things like that.
So, you know, it's an open question, but...
I human aspect of growthx is really important, even though we're an AI company.
Okay.
So knowing that you were somewhat in my position not too long ago, as we were looking for the perfect rule for you, what really brought you to growthx?
be like, hey, this is the next company that I actually want to dive deep into.

Um, that, like, some of the answer I gave was actually, like, part of an answer that Jason, who leads our growth team, gave to me, which is, like, the, you know, it's one thing to work for an AI company, and it's another thing to think about, like, how people interact with AI to get the outputs that they want.
And it's a much more interesting and complicated question.
And so, like, that's one of the reasons I was really excited to work here.
Okay, perfect.
So in terms of the profile,
Looking for, for account strategists, know there's, like, you you mentioned Gritty, kind of helped build the ground up, you know, SEMrush, et cetera.
But, like, what's, like, a successful profile in your eyes, like, having seen what you've seen in your, also, previous roles as well, that would be set up for success, specifically for growthx?
Yeah, somebody who's not afraid to get into the technical side and interact specifically with engineering teams to get what they need for clients.

Because this platform is so new, there are a lot of unknowns, and, like, the asks that we make of engineering to improve the pipelines and workflows that generate the content that we build have to be really specific, and you have to be willing to go back and forth on them and experiment with them.
So, like, you have to, like, part of this role and part of kind of a lot of our client-facing roles are a little bit of, like, UX tester.
You are building the customer experience.
And so taking the time to document and understand the process, both from a technical standpoint and how we're delivering it to clients, is hugely important.
Okay.
I think that's the three main questions that I wanted to ask.
Growthx really intrigues me.
That's why I wanted to continue this conversation.
I really do feel that I'm a strong fit for this role.
Having been in small to more mature startups, I know what I want, and I know how to scale organizations upwards.
And I think that's, hopefully you find this as well, but that's what makes me unique in that sense, especially from an account strategist standpoint.
Of course, there's going be a lot of growing pains I can imagine within Growthx, but I'm here for the ride.
Awesome.
Well, Kyle, was really great to chat with you.
Thank
And I guess we can end this 15 minutes early, but enjoy the rest of your week.
All right, you too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview with Kyle Javier for the Account Strategist role at growthx.ai](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Kyle has 10+ years of experience in customer success, account management, and sales in SaaS, particularly with AI and enterprise search technologies](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=9.0)
  - [The Account Strategist role at growthx.ai is evolving, requiring a blend of technical skills, client management, and the ability to shape the company's transition from services to SaaS](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=48.0)
  - [growthx.ai's platform is new (5-6 weeks old) and leverages multiple AI solutions for content generation at scale](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=153.0)
  - [The ideal candidate needs to be gritty, technically savvy, and able to bridge the gap between clients and engineering teams](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=236.0)

## Topics

### Kyle's Background and Experience

  - [10+ years in SaaS, working with various company sizes (SMB to large enterprise)](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=328.0)
  - [Jack-of-all-trades experience: solutions consultant, sales, business development, customer success](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=340.0)
  - [Recent experience with AI-focused company in enterprise search](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [Philosophy: turn customers into advocates through stories, references, and product co-creation](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=371.0)
  - [Managed strategic accounts like Bass Pro Shops, leading cross-functional projects and turning "red" accounts to "green"](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=429.0)

### growthx.ai Role and Company Overview

  - [Account Strategist role is evolving, not strictly defined as CSM](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=193.0)
  - [Platform is new (5-6 weeks old), leveraging multiple AI solutions for content generation](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=160.0)
  - [Current approach is service-based, with potential transition to SaaS in the future](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=278.0)
  - [Looking for someone to help shape the client-facing motion and provide technical feedback](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=206.0)
  - [Ideal candidate: gritty, scrappy, hungry, willing to build from ground up](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=236.0)
  - [Experience with analytics (e.g., SEMrush, SEO testing) is valuable](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=256.0)

### Client Management and Success Metrics

  - [Kyle's experience includes managing complex, multi-phase projects (e.g., Herbalife across 94 countries)](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=818.0)
  - [Success measured through A/B testing, showcasing value in business reviews, and tracking metrics like year-over-year growth](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=918.0)
  - [Emphasis on constant iteration, collaboration with customers, and understanding their goals](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=1297.0)
  - [Proactive approach to account planning and tiering based on ARR and growth potential](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=1354.0)

### Technical Skills and Tools

  - [Power user of various tools (Slack, ChatGPT Pro) for efficiency and thought leadership](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=1046.0)
  - [Experience in streamlining and automating processes](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=1145.0)
  - [Ability to work closely with engineering teams and provide specific feedback for platform improvements](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=1407.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Andi to evaluate Kyle's fit for the Account Strategist role based on his experience and alignment with growthx.ai's needs](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=1648.0)
  - [Kyle to consider how his experience with enterprise search and AI technologies can translate to growthx.ai's content generation focus](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=1671.0)
  - [Further discussions may be needed to explore Kyle's potential role in shaping growthx.ai's transition from services to SaaS](https://fathom.video/share/LLSWBvjcVuZ4g_xzX51_Fc2GQzdAhWZr?tab=summary&timestamp=1700.0)

action_items.description: Document interview notes/feedback for Kyle Javier's Account Strategist application
